Diagnosis and Management of Barrett's Esophagus: An Updated ACG Guideline
  • Source: Am J Gastroenterol 2022;117:559–587
  • Topic: Barrett's Esophagus (BE) diagnosis, screening, surveillance, and treatment guidelines
  • Author(s): Shaheen NJ, Falk GW, Iyer PG, Souza RF, Yadlapati RH, Sauer BG, Wani S
1. Barrett's Esophagus Overview
  • BE --> metaplastic change replacing squamous epithelium with columnar epithelium containing goblet cells
  • Associated with chronic GERD --> 5%–12% of chronic GERD patients harbor BE
  • Only known precursor lesion to esophageal adenocarcinoma (EAC)
  • EAC incidence --> ↑ rapidly over last 40 years in US and Western countries
2. Diagnosis of BE
2.1 Definition Criteria (Recommendation 1)
  • Requires finding of intestinal metaplasia in tubular esophagus
  • Conditional recommendation, very-low-quality evidence
2.2 Length Requirements (Recommendation 2)
  • Minimum 1 cm columnar mucosa necessary for BE diagnosis
  • Normal-appearing Z line --> no routine endoscopic biopsies
  • Irregular Z line with <1 cm proximal displacement from top of gastric folds --> no routine biopsies
2.3 Biopsy Protocol (Recommendation 3)
  • Minimum 8 endoscopic biopsies required for screening examinations
  • Seattle protocol followed for segments >4 cm
  • Short segments (1–2 cm): at least 4 biopsies per cm circumferential BE + 1 biopsy per cm in tongues
2.4 Dysplasia Confirmation (Recommendation 4)
  • Strong recommendation for second pathologist review
  • Low quality evidence
  • Expert confirmation --> 8-fold ↑ risk of progression vs downstaged patients
2.5 Diagnostic Yield Factors
  • IM yield increases with number of biopsies and segment length
  • 8 biopsies from any given endoscopy --> 94% yield for IM detection
  • Repeat examination may yield 25%+ if initial biopsies negative for IM
3. Screening for BE
3.1 Indications (Recommendation 5)
  • Chronic GERD symptoms + ≥3 additional risk factors
  • Risk factors include:
  • Male sex
  • Age >50 years
  • White race
  • Tobacco smoking
  • Obesity
  • Family history of BE or EAC in first-degree relative
3.2 Risk Factor Prevalence Data
  • BE prevalence without GERD symptoms --> 0.8%
  • Age >50 years --> 6.1% prevalence
  • Male sex --> 6.8% prevalence
  • Obesity --> 1.9% prevalence
  • Family history of BE/EAC --> 23% prevalence
  • GERD + 1 additional risk factor --> 12.2% prevalence
  • Each additional risk factor --> ↑ BE prevalence by 1.2%
3.3 Nonendoscopic Screening (Recommendation 6)
  • Swallowable capsule sponge device with biomarker acceptable alternative
  • Combined with TFF3 or methylated DNA markers (MDMs)
  • Cytosponge-TFF3 --> 10-fold ↑ diagnosis rate vs usual care in pragmatic trial
  • Positive predictive value of Cytosponge-TFF3 --> 59% in screening population
3.4 Repeat Screening (Recommendation 7)
  • Suggest against repeat endoscopic screening after initial negative examination
  • Low quality evidence, conditional recommendation
4. Surveillance of BE
4.1 Endoscopic Techniques (Recommendation 8)
  • Recommend both white light endoscopy AND chromoendoscopy
  • Moderate quality evidence, strong recommendation
  • Acetic acid chromoendoscopy --> pooled sensitivity 92%, specificity 96% for HGD/EAC
  • Narrow band imaging (NBI) targeted biopsies --> pooled sensitivity 94.2%, specificity 94.4% for dysplasia/EAC
4.2 Biopsy Protocol (Recommendation 9)
  • Structured biopsy protocol recommended
  • Seattle protocol --> 4 quadrant biopsies at ≤2 cm intervals from LES to squamocolumnar junction
  • Systematic 4 quadrant protocol --> 13-fold ↑ dysplasia detection vs non-systematic approach
  • Adherence to Seattle protocol worldwide --> only 49%
4.3 Surveillance Intervals Based on Length (Recommendation 11)
  • BE segment <3 cm --> surveillance every 5 years
  • BE segment ≥3 cm --> surveillance every 3 years
  • Moderate quality evidence, strong recommendation
4.4 Dysplasia-Based Surveillance (Recommendation 10)
  • Intervals dictated by degree of dysplasia on previous biopsies
  • Very low quality evidence, conditional recommendation
4.5 Surveillance Intervals Summary Table
Baseline Finding Surveillance Interval
NDBE <3 cm Every 5 years
NDBE ≥3 cm Every 3 years
BE indefinite for dysplasia (confirmed) Repeat EGD within 6 months, then annually if persistent
BE with LGD (surveillance option) 6 months → 12 months → annually
4.6 Progression Rates
  • NDBE annual progression to EAC --> 0.2%–0.05%
  • LGD annual progression to HGD/EAC --> ~0.7% (up to 11.8%/patient-year in RCT)
  • Short-segment BE (<3 cm) annual progression --> 0.06%
  • Long-segment BE (≥3 cm) annual progression --> 0.31%
4.7 WATS-3D Analysis (Recommendation 12)
  • Could not make recommendation on use of wide-area transepithelial sampling with computer-assisted 3D analysis
4.8 Predictive Biomarkers (Recommendation 13)
  • Could not recommend p53 staining or TissueCypher for routine use
  • Low sensitivity and specificity limits clinical implementation
5. Medical Treatment (Non-Endoscopic)
5.1 PPI Therapy (Recommendation 14)
  • Suggest at least once-daily PPI therapy in patients with BE
  • Without allergy or other contraindication to PPI use
  • Very low quality evidence, conditional recommendation
5.2 Chemoprevention Evidence
  • PPI therapy --> 71% reduction in risk of HGD or EAC (adjusted OR 0.29)
  • Time to progression significantly ↓ with PPI use (aHR 0.32)
  • No significant effect with histamine receptor antagonists
5.3 PPI Safety Profile
  • Large RCT (17,598 patients) showed no difference in:
  • All-cause mortality (HR 1.03)
  • Myocardial infarction (HR 0.94)
  • Fractures (OR 0.96)
  • Pneumonia (OR 1.02)
  • Chronic kidney disease (OR 1.17)
  • Dementia (OR 1.2)
  • Statistically significant difference only for enteric infections (OR 1.33)
  • Number needed to harm --> >900 for each year of PPI therapy
5.4 Combination Therapy (Recommendation 15)
  • Could not make recommendation on combination therapy with aspirin and PPI
  • AspECT trial showed high-dose PPI + ASA --> TR 1.59 vs low-dose PPI without ASA
  • Small effect size, driven largely by all-cause mortality reduction
5.5 Antireflux Surgery (Recommendation 16)
  • Suggest against use of antireflux surgery as antineoplastic measure
  • Low quality evidence, conditional recommendation
6. Endoscopic Treatment of BE
6.1 HGD/IMC Management (Recommendation 17)
  • Recommend endoscopic eradication therapy (EET) vs esophagectomy
  • Moderate quality evidence, strong recommendation
  • No difference in 1-, 3-, or 5-year survival between EET and esophagectomy
  • Lower adverse event rates with EET (RR 0.38)
  • Operative mortality of esophagectomy --> 2%
6.2 EET Adverse Events
  • Pooled adverse event rate --> 8.8%
  • Stricture rate --> 5.6%
  • Bleeding rate --> 1%
  • Perforation rate --> 0.6%
6.3 LGD Management (Recommendation 18)
  • Suggest endoscopic therapy to reduce progression risk
  • Endoscopic surveillance remains acceptable alternative
  • Moderate quality evidence, conditional recommendation
6.4 LGD Evidence Summary
  • RFA vs surveillance trial --> progression to HGD/EAC 1.5% vs 26.5%
  • Number needed to treat --> 4 for HGD/EAC prevention
  • RFA reduced EAC alone by 7.4% (NNT 13.6)
6.5 Initial Endoscopic Resection (Recommendation 19)
  • Suggest initial ER of visible lesions before ablative therapy
  • Very low quality evidence, conditional recommendation
6.6 ER Benefits
  • Diagnostic and therapeutic purpose
  • Reduces interobserver variability in dysplasia assessment
  • Changes management in 30%–40% of patients
  • Provides accurate T staging and prognostic information
6.7 ER Techniques
  • Cap-assisted EMR
  • Multiband EMR (preferred in most cases)
  • ESD --> reserved for larger lesions or post-ablation scarring
6.8 Volume Requirements (Recommendation 20)
  • Suggest treatment at high-volume centers
  • Very low quality evidence, conditional recommendation
  • ESGE recommends ≥10 new HGD/early EAC patients per endoscopist annually
6.9 Post-CEIM Surveillance (Recommendation 21)
  • Recommend endoscopic surveillance program after successful EET
  • Moderate quality evidence, strong recommendation
6.10 Post-CEIM Surveillance Intervals
Worst Pretreatment Histology Surveillance Schedule
Low-grade dysplasia 1 yr → 3 yr → every 2 yr thereafter
High-grade dysplasia 3 mo → 6 mo → 12 mo → annually
Intramucosal carcinoma 3 mo → 6 mo → 12 mo → annually
6.11 Recurrence Rates After CEIM
  • Annual incidence of recurrent IM --> 8.6%–10.5%
  • Dysplastic recurrence rate --> 2.0%
  • HGD/EAC recurrence rate --> 1.2%
  • Most recurrences detected at GEJ (75%) and distal esophagus
  • >90% of recurrences can be treated endoscopically
7. Key Concepts for Practice
7.1 Cessation of Surveillance
  • Consider stopping when patient no longer candidate for EET
  • Discuss cessation at age 75 if not occurred earlier
7.2 Quality Indicators
  • Use published quality indicators to benchmark unit performance
  • Monitor CEIM, CED, and adverse event rates
7.3 Cryotherapy Alternative
  • Consider endoscopic cryotherapy for patients unresponsive to RFA
7.4 Informed Consent
  • Patients must have clear understanding of risks and benefits before EET
8. High-Quality Endoscopic Examination (10 Steps)
8.1 Preparation and Landmarks
  • Identify esophageal landmarks (diaphragmatic hiatus, GEJ, squamocolumnar junction)
  • Use distal attachment cap (especially for prior dysplasia cases)
8.2 Inspection Technique
  • Clean mucosa well using water jet channel and suction
  • Use insufflation and desufflation for subtle abnormality detection
  • Spend adequate time in retroflexion view of cardia
8.3 Standardized Reporting
  • Use Prague classification for circumferential and maximal segment length
  • Use Paris classification for superficial neoplasia description
8.4 Biopsy Protocol
  • Use Seattle protocol with partially deflated esophagus
  • 4 quadrant biopsies at ≤2 cm intervals
9. Quality Indicators for EET (Table 8)
9.1 Dysplasia Diagnosis Confirmation
  • Numerator: Patients whose dysplasia diagnosis confirmed by GI pathologist before EET
  • Denominator: All patients receiving EET for dysplasia treatment
  • Threshold --> 90%
9.2 CED Rate by 18 Months
  • Numerator: Patients achieving CED within 18 months
  • Denominator: All patients referred for EET for dysplasia/IMC
  • Threshold --> 80%
9.3 CEIM Rate by 18 Months
  • Numerator: Patients achieving CEIM within 18 months
  • Denominator: All patients referred for EET for dysplasia/IMC
  • Threshold --> 70%
9.4 Adverse Events Tracking
  • Numerator: Adverse events tracked and documented
  • Denominator: All endoscopic procedures involving EET
  • Threshold --> 90%
10. Post-CEIM Surveillance Biopsy Protocol
10.1 GEJ Biopsies
  • Take 4 quadrant biopsies from high cardia just below Z line
  • From top of gastric folds
  • Place in separate biopsy bottle
10.2 Esophageal Recurrence Zone
  • Take 4 quadrant biopsies from distal 2–5 cm of neosquamous epithelium
  • Most recurrences within 5 cm of distal esophagus (87%)
  • Low yield from normal neosquamous epithelium above this zone
10.3 Utility of Random Biopsies
  • Yield from normal-appearing neosquamous epithelium --> 1% for any recurrence
  • Yield for dysplastic recurrence from normal neosquamous --> 0.2%
  • Utility likely limited to distal 2–4 cm of tubular esophagus
11. Advanced Imaging Technologies
11.1 Confocal Laser Endomicroscopy
  • Blue laser light with IV fluorescein for real-time in vivo imaging
  • Pooled sensitivity --> 89%
  • Pooled specificity --> 83%
  • Considerable capital cost and training requirements
11.2 Volumetric Laser Endomicroscopy
  • Optical coherence tomography technology
  • 6 cm circumferential scan, depth of 3 mm
  • Not commercially available currently
11.3 Artificial Intelligence Systems
  • Deep learning CADe systems --> superior to nonexpert endoscopists
  • Validated for dysplasia/neoplasia detection in Barrett's esophagus
  • Pilot studies show promising live endoscopy results
12. Nonendoscopic Detection Devices Performance
12.1 Cytosponge with TFF3 (UK)
  • Case-control study: Cases 647, Controls 463
  • Sensitivity --> 80%
  • Specificity --> 92%
12.2 Cytosponge with TFF3 (US)
  • Case-control study: Cases 129, Controls 62
  • Sensitivity --> 76%
  • Specificity --> 77%
12.3 EsophaCap with MDMs (25mm)
  • Cases 112, Controls 89
  • Sensitivity --> 92%
  • Specificity --> 94%
12.4 EsoCheck with MDMs
  • Cases 50, Controls 36
  • Sensitivity --> 92%
  • Specificity --> 88%
13. Key Changes from Previous Guidelines
13.1 Screening Modalities
  • Broadened acceptable screening to include nonendoscopic methods
13.2 Surveillance Intervals
  • Liberalized intervals for short-segment BE (<3 cm) --> now 5 years
13.3 Volume Criteria
  • Added volume criteria for endoscopic therapy centers for BE
13.4 Key Omissions from Recommendations
  • Could not make recommendations on chemoprevention
  • Could not recommend routine biomarker use
14. Pathologist Expertise and Discordance
14.1 Diagnostic Concordance Rates
  • Nondysplastic BE --> 79% concordance among pathologists
  • HGD --> 71% concordance
  • LGD --> 42% concordance
  • Indefinite for dysplasia --> 23% concordance
14.2 Expert Pathologist Features
  • ≥5 years professional experience --> protective against major diagnostic errors (OR 0.48)
  • Working in nonteaching hospital --> ↑ odds of major diagnostic errors (OR 1.76)
  • Case volume or self-identifying as expert --> did not predict diagnostic proficiency
14.3 LGD Confirmation Impact
  • Dutch cohort: 73% of community LGD diagnoses downgraded by expert panel
  • Confirmed LGD progression --> 9.1%/patient-year
  • Downgraded to IND --> 0.9%/patient-year
  • Downgraded to NDBE --> 0.6%/patient-year
15. Post-Endoscopy Cancer Rates
15.1 Definitions
  • Post-endoscopy EAC --> diagnosis within first year after index endoscopy showing NDBE/LGD/IND
15.2 Meta-Analysis Findings
  • Proportion of post-endoscopy EAC --> 21% (95% CI 13–31)
  • Proportion of post-endoscopy HGD/EAC --> 26% (95% CI 19–34)
  • NDBE-only patients: post-endoscopy EAC --> 17%, HGD/EAC --> 14%
15.3 Implications
  • Strong inverse association between post-endoscopy EAC and incident EAC
  • Questions effectiveness of current screening/surveillance practices
  • Highlights importance of high-quality endoscopy
16. Barrett's Length and Cancer Risk
16.1 Meta-Analysis Data
  • Each cm increase in segment length --> OR 1.25 (95% CI 1.16–1.36) for progression
  • Short-segment (<3 cm) vs long-segment (≥3 cm):
  • EAC alone: 0.06% vs 0.31%
  • HGD + EAC combined: 0.24% vs 0.76%
16.2 Multicenter Cohort Data
  • Segments <3 cm --> lower annual progression to EAC or HGD/EAC: 0.07% vs 0.25%
  • Short-segment progression to combined endpoint: 0.29% vs 0.91%
17. Endoscopic Resection Techniques Comparison
17.1 EMR vs ESD
  • Both cap-assisted and multiband EMR have equal efficacy and safety
  • Cap-assisted EMR may allow slightly larger tissue samples
  • Multiband EMR preferred in most cases
17.2 ESD Considerations
  • Steeper learning curve
  • Requires more time to complete
  • Higher complication rates
  • No formal training pathways in the West
  • May be preferred for:
  • Larger lesions unsuitable for en bloc EMR
  • Lesions with potential submucosal invasion
  • Post-ablation scarring
17.3 EMR Followed by Ablation vs Stepwise Radical EMR
  • RFA reached CEIM faster with fewer procedures and complications
  • Stepwise radical EMR had more complications
18. Cryotherapy Outcomes
18.1 Liquid Nitrogen Spray Cryotherapy
  • CED rates --> 81%–88%
  • CEIM rates --> 57%–61%
18.2 Refractory to RFA
  • CED --> 76%
  • CEIM --> 46%
18.3 C2 Cryoballoon Ablation
  • CED --> 76%
  • CEIM --> 72% (intention-to-treat analysis)
18.4 Stricture Rate
  • Associated with 9%–12% stricture rate
19. Volume-Outcome Relationships
19.1 US RFA Registry Data
  • Treatment sessions to achieve CEIM ↓ with increasing volume
  • Relationship attenuates after treating ≥30 patients by center or endoscopist
19.2 CEIM Rates and Volume
  • Retrospective study showed significant correlation between RFA volume and CEIM rates
19.3 Recurrence and Volume
  • High-volume RFA facilities --> reduced recurrence risk (HR 0.19 vs low-volume)
  • Endoscopist procedure volume --> independent predictor of adverse outcomes
20. Key Statistical Thresholds and Definitions
20.1 CEIM Definition
  • Variable in literature: 1 or 2 surveillance endoscopies negative for visible BE and IM
20.2 Recurrence Definition
  • Detection of IM (with or without dysplasia) from tubular esophagus or GEJ after achieving CEIM
20.3 Timing of Recurrence
  • Most recurrences in first year after CEIM (studies using 1 negative biopsy definition)
  • Peak at 18 months in some studies
  • Continue to increase up to 5 years for both nondysplastic and dysplastic BE
20.4 Location of Recurrence
  • Most recurrences at GEJ (75%)
  • Remainder in tubular esophagus
  • 60% of GEJ recurrences not visible to endoscopist
  • 80% of tubular esophagus recurrences visible
21. Clinical Decision Making Framework
21.1 Dysplasia Management Algorithm
  • HGD/IMC --> EET recommended (strong)
  • LGD --> Shared decision making between EET and surveillance
  • NDBE <3 cm --> Surveillance every 5 years
  • NDBE ≥3 cm --> Surveillance every 3 years
21.2 Shared Decision Making Elements
  • Diagnosis confirmed by expert pathologist
  • Patient understands risks/benefits of EET vs surveillance
  • Progression risk discussed (1.5%–26.5% depending on confirmation)
  • Patient preferences and comorbidities considered
21.3 When to Consider Cessation
  • Estimated survival <5 years
  • No longer fit for repeated endoscopy
  • Cannot tolerate endoscopic, surgical, or oncological intervention
22. Endoscopic Examination Quality Benchmarks
22.1 Inspection Time
  • Longer inspection time --> ↑ detection of HGD/EAC
22.2 Seattle Protocol Adherence
  • Community-based database: only 51.2% adherence
  • Nonadherence inversely associated with increasing BE length
22.3 Dysplasia Detection Impact
  • Systematic vs targeted biopsies: 73% vs 27% yield of dysplasia/EAC
  • Nonadherence associated with significantly ↓ dysplasia detection (OR 0.53)
23. Risk Stratification Models
23.1 Clinical Prediction Model
  • Incorporates: segment length, male sex, cigarette smoking, LGD
  • Categorizes patients as low, intermediate, or high risk for progression
23.2 Validation
  • Validated in population-based cohort from Northern Ireland
23.3 Biomarker Integration
  • Barrett's progression score incorporating clinical and biomarker variables proposed
  • No study yet evaluated combination of clinical + biomarker variables
24. Biomarker Performance Characteristics
24.1 p53 Immunohistochemistry
  • OR for progression in case-control studies --> 4–17
  • LGD cohort studies: RR 14.25 (95% CI 6.76–30.02)
  • Sensitivity for overexpression --> 62%
  • Specificity for overexpression --> 80%
24.2 TissueCypher Assay
  • NDBE patients: sensitivity 30.4%, specificity 95% at 5 years
  • LGD patients (2-tiered): sensitivity 67.7%, specificity 78.6%
  • Prevalent HGD/EAC detection: OR 46 (95% CI 14.86–169)
24.3 Limitations
  • Low sensitivity and specificity for routine clinical use
  • Not even perfect biomarker eliminates cancer development/deaths
25. Economic Considerations
25.1 Screening Cost-Effectiveness
  • Assumes 100% participation and 100% accuracy --> overestimates potential
  • Acceptable ICERs: $10,000–$50,000/QALY gained
25.2 WATS-3D Cost-Effectiveness
  • At $100,000/QALY --> 98.7% of simulated trials cost-effective
  • At $150,000/QALY --> 100%
25.3 Cytosponge Cost-Effectiveness
  • Cost-effective when compared with no screening
  • In 50-year-old White men with chronic reflux
26. Meta-Analysis Quality Assessment Framework (GRADE)
26.1 Evidence Quality Factors
  • Factors lowering quality: Imprecision, risk of bias, inconsistency, indirectness, publication bias
  • Factors increasing quality: Dose-response gradient, large effect, plausible confounding
26.2 Evidence Levels
  • High --> confident in effect estimate
  • Moderate --> moderately confident
  • Low --> limited confidence
  • Very low --> very little confidence
26.3 Recommendation Strengths
  • Strong (recommendations) --> most patients should receive recommended course
  • Conditional (suggestions) --> many patients should have this, but different choices appropriate for some
27. Key Statistical Outcomes from Clinical Trials
27.1 AspECT Trial Results
  • High-dose PPI (40 mg twice daily) vs low-dose (20 mg once daily): TR 1.27 (95% CI 1.01–1.58, P=0.038)
  • ASA vs no ASA: TR 1.24 (95% CI 0.98–1.57) - not significant
  • Censoring for NSAIDs: ASA better than no ASA: TR 1.29 (95% CI 1.01–1.66, P=0.04)
  • High-dose PPI + ASA vs low-dose PPI without ASA: TR 1.59 (95% CI 1.14–2.23, P=0.006)
27.2 SURF Trial Results
  • RFA vs surveillance in LGD: 1.5% vs 26.5% progression to HGD/EAC
  • EAC alone: 1.5% vs 8.8%
  • Number needed to treat for HGD/EAC prevention --> 4
27.3 RFA Registry Outcomes
  • Most patients achieve CEIM within 3 endoscopy sessions
  • Recurrence rare after complete eradication
28. Endoscopic Landmark Identification
28.1 Prague Classification Components
  • Circumferential extent (C): length of circumferential involvement
  • Maximal extent (M): longest tongue of BE
28.2 Reliability Coefficients
  • Circumferential extent --> 0.95 (almost perfect)
  • Maximal extent --> 0.94 (almost perfect)
  • Segments <1 cm --> 0.22 (only fair)
28.3 Clinical Implications
  • Fair reliability for small segments --> led to 1 cm threshold recommendation
  • Most professional societies require this threshold
29. GERD Symptom-Based Screening Limitations
29.1 Population Without Chronic Reflux
  • ~40%–50% of EAC patients do not endorse chronic reflux
  • 34% of EAC diagnosed in those without chronic GERD symptoms (SEER-based model)
29.2 Sensitivity/Specificity Issues
  • Current guidelines requiring reflux symptoms: sensitivity 39%–43%, specificity 67%–76%
  • Limiting to chronic reflux reduces targeting of at-risk population by ~50%
29.3 Population Expansion
  • Removing reflux symptom requirement: 120 million adults >40 years vs 30 million with chronic reflux
  • Cost-effectiveness and practical implications largely unknown
30. Endoscopic Therapy Center Requirements
30.1 Minimum Volume Recommendations
  • ESGE recommends ≥10 new patients with HGD or early EAC per endoscopist annually
30.2 Center Requirements
  • High volume of BE-related neoplasia patients
  • Trained endoscopists in advanced imaging, EMR, and ablation
  • Adherence to standardized protocol with quality monitoring
30.3 Multidisciplinary Team Access
  • Expert GI pathologists
  • Surgeons
  • Medical oncologists
  • Radiation oncologists
31. Post-CEIM Surveillance Algorithm Details
31.1 Biopsy Location Rationale
  • GEJ biopsies in separate bottle (detect cardia recurrence)
  • Distal 2–5 cm esophageal biopsies in separate bottle
  • Most recurrences within 5 cm of distal esophagus (87%)
31.2 Yield Considerations
  • Random biopsies from normal neosquamous epithelium --> low yield (1% any recurrence, 0.2% dysplastic)
  • Utility likely limited to distal 2–4 cm of tubular esophagus
31.3 Timing Based on Pre-Ablation Histology
  • LGD: less frequent surveillance (1 yr, 3 yr, then every 2 yr)
  • HGD/IMC: more intensive surveillance (3 mo intervals initially)
32. Endoscopic Treatment Algorithm Summary
32.1 Patient Evaluation Steps
  • High-definition white light endoscopy + virtual chromoendoscopy
  • Initiation of twice-daily PPI therapy
  • Careful inspection for visible lesions
32.2 Visible Lesion Management
  • Always resect before ablative therapy
  • Mucosal resection may upstage BE
32.3 Ablation of Residual BE
  • After successful ER of visible abnormalities, ablation recommended regardless of histology
  • RFA preferred ablative technique
32.4 Follow-Up Based on Staging
  • Algorithm for most severe histology if upstaged by ER
33. Evidence Summary Tables Reference
33.1 GRADE Quality Assessment (Table 1)
  • Study design determines initial quality level
  • Factors can lower or raise quality assessment
33.2 Recommendations Summary (Table 2)
  • 21 total recommendations
  • Quality of evidence ranges from very low to moderate
  • Strength ranges from conditional to strong
33.3 Key Concepts (Table 3)
  • Statements without GRADE application
  • Often definitions or epidemiological statements
34. Clinical Practice Application Framework
34.1 Diagnosis Verification
  • Confirm BE diagnosis requires IM on biopsy
  • Minimum 1 cm segment length
  • ≥8 biopsies for screening
34.2 Screening Decision
  • Chronic GERD + ≥3 risk factors --> consider screening
  • Nonendoscopic options available (Cytosponge, EsophaCap)
34.3 Surveillance Assignment
  • Based on segment length and dysplasia status
  • Follow structured intervals
34.4 Treatment Selection
  • HGD/IMC --> EET recommended
  • LGD --> shared decision making
  • NDBE --> surveillance per length
34.5 Post-Treatment Monitoring
  • CEIM achieved --> structured surveillance based on pre-ablation histology
  • Monitor quality indicators
35. Future Directions and Research Needs
35.1 Screening Optimization
  • Refinement of risk prediction algorithms
  • Validation of nonendoscopic device performance
35.2 Surveillance Refinement
  • Optimal intervals and tissue acquisition methods
  • Better biomarkers for risk stratification
35.3 Artificial Intelligence Applications
  • Dysplasia detection during endoscopy
  • Pathologic interpretation assistance
35.4 Quality Metric Development
  • Continued refinement of quality indicators
  • Implementation in training curricula
35.5 Post-Endoscopy Cancer Prevention
  • Understanding causes of post-endoscopy EAC
  • Strategies to reduce missed lesions
36. Guideline Implementation Considerations
36.1 Patient-Centered Approach
  • Incorporate patient-specific comorbidities and preferences
  • Shared decision making for treatment selection
36.2 Resource Availability
  • Access to high-volume centers may be limited
  • Training and infrastructure needs
36.3 Equity Concerns
  • Racial screening criteria acknowledge social construct limitations
  • Need for more precise genetic variants
36.4 Cost-Benefit Analysis
  • Screening programs require significant resources
  • Nonendoscopic options may improve cost-effectiveness
37. Summary of Major Recommendations by Category
37.1 Diagnosis (4 recommendations)
  • IM required for BE diagnosis
  • ≥1 cm segment length minimum
  • ≥8 biopsies with Seattle protocol
  • Expert pathology confirmation for dysplasia
37.2 Screening (3 recommendations)
  • Single endoscopy screening for chronic GERD + ≥3 risk factors
  • Nonendoscopic capsule device acceptable alternative
  • Against repeat screening after initial negative
37.3 Surveillance (6 recommendations)
  • White light + chromoendoscopy recommended
  • Structured biopsy protocol required
  • Surveillance intervals based on dysplasia and length
  • No recommendation on WATS-3D or biomarkers
37.4 Medical Treatment (3 recommendations)
  • At least once-daily PPI for all BE patients
  • No recommendation on ASA + PPI combination
  • Against antireflux surgery as antineoplastic measure
37.5 Endoscopic Treatment (5 recommendations)
  • EET recommended for HGD/IMC
  • EET suggested for LGD to reduce progression
  • Initial ER of visible lesions before ablation
  • Treatment at high-volume centers suggested
  • Post-CEIM surveillance program recommended
38. Clinical Algorithm Summary
38.1 Columnar-Lined Esophagus Found
  • Measure segment length (Prague criteria)
  • If ≥1 cm --> obtain ≥8 biopsies
  • If <1 cm with normal appearance --> no routine biopsies
38.2 Screening Positive
  • Confirm diagnosis with IM on biopsy
  • If confirmed --> enter surveillance program
38.3 Surveillance Findings
  • NDBE <3 cm --> 5-year intervals
  • NDBE ≥3 cm --> 3-year intervals
  • LGD confirmed --> shared decision for EET vs surveillance
  • HGD/IMC --> EET recommended
38.4 Post-CEIM Surveillance
  • Based on pre-ablation histology
  • LGD: 1 yr, 3 yr, then every 2 yr
  • HGD/IMC: 3 mo, 6 mo, 12 mo, then annually
39. Quality Improvement Metrics
39.1 Process Measures
  • Dysplasia diagnosis by expert pathologist (target 90%)
  • Adverse events tracking documentation (target 90%)
39.2 Outcome Measures
  • CED by 18 months (target 80%)
  • CEIM by 18 months (target 70%)
39.3 Performance Gap Identification
  • Compare individual/center performance to benchmarks
  • Implement quality improvement interventions
40. Patient Counseling Key Points
40.1 Diagnosis Education
  • BE is a precursor to EAC but cancer risk is low for most patients
  • Surveillance allows early detection of progression
40.2 Treatment Options
  • EET highly effective for dysplasia/neoplasia
  • Risks and benefits should be discussed
40.3 Surveillance Compliance
  • Importance of adherence to recommended intervals
  • Risks of missed progression with non-adherence
40.4 Lifestyle Considerations
  • PPI therapy typically recommended
  • Antireflux surgery not recommended for cancer prevention
41. Documentation Requirements
41.1 Endoscopic Report Elements
  • Prague classification for BE extent
  • Paris classification for any lesions
  • Landmarks identified (GEJ, diaphragmatic hiatus)
  • Biopsy protocol used
41.2 Pathology Communication
  • Request expert review for dysplasia diagnoses
  • Document second pathologist confirmation
41.3 Treatment Planning
  • Record shared decision making discussion
  • Document patient preferences and comorbidities
41.4 Follow-Up Tracking
  • Schedule appropriate surveillance appointments
  • Track completion of recommended intervals
42. Research Gaps and Future Directions
42.1 Biomarker Validation
  • Prospective studies needed for p53 and TissueCypher
  • Integration with clinical risk models
42.2 Screening Optimization
  • Optimal risk stratification algorithms
  • Validation of nonendoscopic devices in diverse populations
42.3 Surveillance Interval Refinement
  • Evidence-based intervals for different risk categories
  • Role of advanced imaging in surveillance
42.4 Treatment Outcomes
  • Long-term data on CEIM durability
  • Comparison of ablation techniques
42.5 Quality Improvement
  • Implementation studies for quality metrics
  • Impact on patient outcomes
43. Guideline Update Process Notes
43.1 Methodology
  • GRADE process for evidence assessment
  • PICO format for clinical questions
  • 21 clinically relevant questions developed
43.2 Evidence Selection
  • MEDLINE and PubMed literature searches
  • Emphasis on meta-analyses and RCTs when available
  • Abstracts and case reports excluded
43.3 Recommendation Grading
  • Quality of evidence: high, moderate, low, very low
  • Strength of recommendation: strong (recommendation) or conditional (suggestion)
43.4 Limitations Acknowledged
  • Reliance on retrospective studies for many recommendations
  • Need for RCTs in some areas
44. Clinical Practice Update Summary
44.1 Major Changes from Prior Guidelines
  • Nonendoscopic screening modalities now acceptable
  • Short-segment BE surveillance interval extended to 5 years
  • Volume criteria for EET centers established
  • Post-CEIM surveillance intervals updated
44.2 Areas Without Recommendations
  • Chemoprevention (insufficient data)
  • Routine biomarker use (insufficient data)
44.3 Key Clinical Decision Points
  • Diagnosis confirmation with IM and segment measurement
  • Risk stratification for screening candidates
  • Dysplasia management pathway selection
  • Post-treatment surveillance scheduling
45. Implementation Resources
45.1 Quality Indicators
  • Use published benchmarks for performance assessment
  • Track CEIM, CED, and adverse event rates
45.2 Training Programs
  • Advanced imaging interpretation
  • EMR and ablation techniques
  • Standardized protocols
45.3 Institutional Support
  • Multidisciplinary team access
  • Pathology expertise availability
  • Quality improvement infrastructure
45.4 Patient Education Materials
  • BE and cancer risk explanation
  • Surveillance importance
  • Treatment option comparisons
46. Summary of Key Statistics for Quick Reference
Metric Value
BE prevalence in chronic GERD 5%–12%
Sensitivity of Cytosponge-TFF3 80%–92%
Specificity of Cytosponge-TFF3 77%–94%
Short-segment BE progression rate 0.06%/year
Long-segment BE progression rate 0.31%/year
Minimum biopsies for diagnosis 8
Seattle protocol adherence (global) 49%
CEIM typically achieved in 3 sessions
Post-CEIM recurrence rate 8.6%–10.5%/year
47. Abbreviations and Definitions
47.1 Common Abbreviations
  • BE: Barrett's esophagus
  • EAC: esophageal adenocarcinoma
  • GERD: gastroesophageal reflux disease
  • IM: intestinal metaplasia
  • HGD: high-grade dysplasia
  • LGD: low-grade dysplasia
  • NDBE: nondysplastic Barrett's esophagus
  • EET: endoscopic eradication therapy
  • RFA: radiofrequency ablation
  • EMR: endoscopic mucosal resection
  • ESD: endoscopic submucosal dissection
  • CEIM: complete eradication of intestinal metaplasia
  • CED: complete eradication of dysplasia
  • PPI: proton pump inhibitor
  • ASA: aspirin
  • NBI: narrow band imaging
47.2 Key Definitions
  • CEIM: 1 or 2 surveillance endoscopies negative for visible BE and IM on biopsies
  • Recurrence: Detection of IM (with or without dysplasia) from tubular esophagus or GEJ after CEIM
  • Prague C&M criteria: Standardized endoscopic grading system for BE extent
48. Clinical Practice Checklist
48.1 Diagnosis Verification
  • [ ] Segment ≥1 cm confirmed
  • [ ] IM present on biopsy
  • [ ] ≥8 biopsies obtained
  • [ ] Expert pathology review for dysplasia
48.2 Screening Assessment
  • [ ] Chronic GERD symptoms present
  • [ ] Risk factors documented (≥3 for screening)
  • [ ] Patient counseled on screening options
48.3 Surveillance Protocol
  • [ ] Segment length measured (Prague)
  • [ ] Dysplasia status confirmed
  • [ ] Interval assigned based on length/dysplasia
  • [ ] Patient understands follow-up schedule
48.4 Treatment Planning (Dysplasia)
  • [ ] Expert pathology confirmation obtained
  • [ ] Shared decision making documented
  • [ ] Treatment at high-volume center confirmed
  • [ ] Risks and benefits discussed
48.5 Post-CEIM Follow-Up
  • [ ] Surveillance scheduled per pre-ablation histology
  • [ ] GEJ biopsies in separate bottle
  • [ ] Distal esophageal biopsies obtained
  • [ ] Quality metrics tracked
49. Evidence-Based Decision Points Summary
49.1 When to Screen
  • Chronic GERD + ≥3 risk factors --> consider screening
  • Single endoscopy recommended
49.2 When to Perform Biopsy
  • Segment ≥1 cm with columnar mucosa
  • Normal Z line without visible lesions --> no routine biopsy
49.3 When to Treat
  • HGD or IMC confirmed --> EET recommended
  • LGD confirmed --> shared decision for treatment vs surveillance
49.4 When to Extend Intervals
  • NDBE <3 cm with adequate baseline exam --> 5-year surveillance acceptable
49.5 When to Stop Surveillance
  • Patient no longer candidate for EET
  • Estimated survival <5 years
50. Final Summary: Guideline Core Principles
50.1 Diagnosis
  • Require IM and ≥1 cm segment length for BE diagnosis
  • Obtain adequate biopsies with expert pathology review
50.2 Screening
  • Target patients with chronic GERD and multiple risk factors
  • Nonendoscopic options now available as acceptable alternatives
50.3 Surveillance
  • Base intervals on segment length and dysplasia status
  • Use high-quality endoscopy with chromoendoscopy
50.4 Treatment
  • EET preferred over surgery for dysplasia/neoplasia
  • Perform at high-volume centers with expert teams
50.5 Follow-Up
  • Structured surveillance after CEIM based on pre-ablation histology
  • Monitor quality indicators and improve performance
